**Petróleo Brasileiro S.A.**, better known by and [trading as](/wiki/Trade_name "Trade name") the [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") **Petrobras** (Portuguese pronunciation: [[ˌpɛtɾoˈbɾas, pet-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")), is a Brazilian [majority state-owned](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") [multinational corporation](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") in the [petroleum industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry"), which is headquartered in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). The company's name translates to **Brazilian Petroleum Corporation**.

The company was ranked #71 in the 2023 [*Fortune* Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500") list.[[9]](#cite_note-10) In the 2023 [*Forbes* Global 2000](/wiki/Forbes_Global_2000 "Forbes Global 2000"), Petrobras was ranked as the 58th-largest public company in the world.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Petrobras_revenue_2002-2006.jpg/250px-Petrobras_revenue_2002-2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petrobras_revenue_2002-2006.jpg)

Petrobras' [financial growth](/wiki/Financial_growth "Financial growth") between 2002 and 2006

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Petrobr%C3%A1s-cavalo-mec%C3%A2nico-3.jpg/250px-Petrobr%C3%A1s-cavalo-mec%C3%A2nico-3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petrobr%C3%A1s-cavalo-mec%C3%A2nico-3.jpg)

Petrobras standard model for its land oil pump, popularly known as Wooden Horse (*Cavalo de Pau* in Portuguese) in [UFRN](/wiki/Universidade_Federal_do_Rio_Grande_do_Norte "Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Norte"), [Natal](/wiki/Natal,_Brazil "Natal, Brazil"), Brazil.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Building_in_Avenida_Paulista%2C_Brazil_2.jpg/250px-Building_in_Avenida_Paulista%2C_Brazil_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Building_in_Avenida_Paulista,_Brazil_2.jpg)

Skyscraper hosting Petrobras' offices in [Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue"), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo").

Petrobras was created in 1953 under the [government](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Get%C3%BAlio_Vargas "Second presidency of Getúlio Vargas") of Brazilian president [Getúlio Vargas](/wiki/Get%C3%BAlio_Vargas "Getúlio Vargas") with the slogan "The Oil is Ours" (Portuguese: "O petróleo é nosso"). It was given a legal monopoly in Brazil.[[11]](#cite_note-12) In 1953, Brazil produced only 2,700 barrels of oil per day.[[12]](#cite_note-wapo-13) In 1961, the company's REDUC refinery began operations near Rio de Janeiro,[[13]](#cite_note-14) and in 1963, its [Cenpes](/wiki/Cenpes "Cenpes") research center opened in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"); it remains one of the world's largest centers dedicated to energy research.[[14]](#cite_note-15) In 1968, the company established Petrobras Quimica S.A ("Petroquisa"), a subsidiary focused on [petrochemicals](/wiki/Petrochemical "Petrochemical") and the conversion of [naphtha](/wiki/Naphtha "Naphtha") into [ethene](/wiki/Ethene "Ethene").[[15]](#cite_note-16)

Petrobras had begun processing [oil shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale") in 1953, developing the [Petrosix](/wiki/Petrosix "Petrosix") technology for extracting oil from oil shale. It began using an industrial-size [retort](/wiki/Retort "Retort") to process shale in the 1990s.[[16]](#cite_note-17) In 2006, Petrobras said that their industrial retort could process 260 tonnes/hour of oil shale.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

In 1994, Petrobras put the [Petrobras 36](/wiki/Petrobras_36 "Petrobras 36"), the world's largest [oil platform](/wiki/Oil_platform "Oil platform"), into service. It sank after an explosion in 2001 and was a complete loss.[[18]](#cite_note-19) In 1997, the government approved Law N.9.478, which broke Petrobras's monopoly and allowed competition in Brazil's oilfields, and also created the [national petroleum agency](/wiki/National_Agency_of_Petroleum,_Natural_Gas_and_Biofuels_(Brazil) "National Agency of Petroleum, Natural Gas and Biofuels (Brazil)"), Agência Nacional do Petróleo (ANP), responsible for the regulation and supervision of the petroleum industry, and the National Council of Energy Policies, a public agency responsible for developing public energy policy.[[19]](#cite_note-brazilbusiness-20) In 1999, the National Petroleum Agency signed agreements with other companies, ending the company's monopoly.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

In 2000, Petrobras set a world record for oil exploration in deep waters, reaching a depth of 1,877 metres (6,158 ft) below sea level.[[21]](#cite_note-22) In 2002, Petrobras acquired the Argentine company Perez Companc Energía (PECOM Energía S.A.) from the [Perez Companc Family Group](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perez_Companc_Family_Group "es:Perez Companc Family Group") and its family foundation for $1.18 billion. This acquisition included assets in [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), Brazil, [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), and [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), 1.1 billion barrels of crude oil reserves, and production of 181 thousand [barrels of oil equivalent](/wiki/Barrel_of_oil_equivalent "Barrel of oil equivalent") (1,110,000 GJ) per day.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

In 2005, Petrobras announced a [joint venture](/wiki/Joint_venture "Joint venture") with Nippon Alcohol Hanbai KK to sell Brazilian [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") to Japan, called Brazil-Japan Ethanol.[[23]](#cite_note-24) On 21 April 2006, the company started production on the P-50 oil platform in the [Albacora-Leste oil field](/wiki/Albacora-Leste_oil_field "Albacora-Leste oil field") at Campos Basin, which made Brazil self-sufficient in oil production.[[12]](#cite_note-wapo-13) During the administration of the [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)") in the 2010s, Petrobras spent an estimated $40 billion subsidizing motor fuels, causing it to become the world's most indebted oil company.[[24]](#cite_note-25) By November 2015, the company had accumulated $128 billion in debt, 84% of it denominated in foreign currencies.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

In 2022, under the administration of [Caio Paes de Andrade](/wiki/Caio_Paes_de_Andrade "Caio Paes de Andrade"), Petrobras achieved its highest net profit in history, totaling R$188.3 billion, distributing R$215.7 billion in dividends, making it the second largest dividend payer in the world.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

In summer 2023, Petrobras entered into a 5-year agreement with [Weatherford International](/wiki/Weatherford_International "Weatherford International") for providing intervention services [offshore](/wiki/Offshore_construction "Offshore construction") [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). As part of the cooperation, the US company will also provide [digitalization](/wiki/Digital_transformation "Digital transformation") of production using the Centro well construction optimization platform.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Also in 2023, Petrobras announced the resumption of construction of a new production line at the [Abreu e Lima](/wiki/Abreu_e_Lima "Abreu e Lima") [refinery](/wiki/Refinery "Refinery") in [Ipojuca](/wiki/Ipojuca "Ipojuca"), [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). The initial investment in the refinery is estimated at $18.5 billion, and the refinery will refine [heavy crude oil](/wiki/Heavy_crude_oil "Heavy crude oil") from the Pre-Salt region.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

## Operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Operations")]

### Business areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Business areas")]

The company operates in six business areas, listed in order of revenue:[[2]](#cite_note-20F-2)

* Refining, transportation and marketing – refining, [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics"), transportation, trading operations, oil products and crude oil exports and imports and petrochemical investments in Brazil
* Exploration and production – crude oil, [natural gas liquids](/wiki/Natural_gas_liquids "Natural gas liquids") (NGL) and natural gas exploration, development and production in Brazil
* Distribution – distribution of oil products, [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") to [wholesalers](/wiki/Wholesaling "Wholesaling") and through the Petrobras Distribuidora S.A. retail network in Brazil
* Gas and power – transportation and trading of natural gas and LNG, and generation and trading of electric power, and the [fertilizer](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer") business
* International – exploration and production of oil and gas, refining, transportation and marketing, distribution and gas and power operations outside of Brazil
* Biofuels – production of [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") and its co-products and ethanol-related activities such as [equity](/wiki/Equity_(finance) "Equity (finance)") investments, production and trading of [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), sugar and the excess electricity generated from sugarcane [bagasse](/wiki/Bagasse "Bagasse")

### Production and reserves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Production and reserves")]

Petrobras controls significant oil and energy assets in 16 countries in Africa, North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.[[2]](#cite_note-20F-2)

However, Brazil represented 92% of Petrobras' worldwide production in 2014 and accounted for 97% of Petrobras' worldwide reserves on 31 December 2014,[[2]](#cite_note-20F-2) when the company had 8,112.8 million [barrels of oil equivalent](/wiki/Barrel_of_oil_equivalent "Barrel of oil equivalent") (4.9633×1010 GJ) of proved developed reserves and 4,599.7 million [barrels of oil equivalent](/wiki/Barrel_of_oil_equivalent "Barrel of oil equivalent") (2.8140×1010 GJ) of proved undeveloped reserves in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil").[[2]](#cite_note-20F-2) Of these, 62.7% were located in the offshore [Campos Basin](/wiki/Campos_Basin "Campos Basin").[[2]](#cite_note-20F-2) The largest growth prospect for the company is the [Tupi oil field](/wiki/Tupi_oil_field "Tupi oil field") in the [Santos Basin](/wiki/Santos_Basin "Santos Basin").[[2]](#cite_note-20F-2)

In 2015, the company produced 2.284 million [barrels of oil equivalent](/wiki/Barrel_of_oil_equivalent "Barrel of oil equivalent") (13,970,000 GJ) per day, of which 89% was [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") and 11% was [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas").[[2]](#cite_note-20F-2)

### Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Production")]

In 1961, Petrobras geologist [Walter K. Link](/wiki/Walter_K._Link "Walter K. Link") published [Link's memorandum](/wiki/Link%27s_memorandum "Link's memorandum"), which implied that the company was better off exploring offshore instead of onshore.[[29]](#cite_note-30) In 1963, Petrobras discovered the [Recôncavo Baiano](/wiki/Rec%C3%B4ncavo_Baiano "Recôncavo Baiano") and [Carmópolis](/wiki/Carm%C3%B3polis "Carmópolis") oil fields.[[19]](#cite_note-brazilbusiness-20)

The company's growth was halted by the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"). The entire country was affected, and the "Brazilian miracle", a period when annual [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") growth exceeded 10%, ended. Petrobras nearly went bankrupt.[[30]](#cite_note-31) In 1974, the company discovered an oil field in the [Campos Basin](/wiki/Campos_Basin "Campos Basin"). This discovery boosted its finances and helped it restructure nationwide.[[31]](#cite_note-32) In 1975, the [Brazilian Government](/wiki/Brazilian_Government "Brazilian Government") temporarily allowed foreign operators into Brazil, and Petrobras signed exploration contracts with foreign companies for oilfields in Brazil.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The company was affected by the [1979 energy crisis](/wiki/1979_energy_crisis "1979 energy crisis"), although not nearly as badly as in 1973.

In 1997, Petrobras reached the production milestone of 1 million barrels (160,000 m3) per day. The company also executed agreements with other Latin American governments and began operations outside Brazil.[[33]](#cite_note-wharton-34)

In 2003, on its 50th anniversary, Petrobras surpassed 2 million [barrels of oil equivalent](/wiki/Barrel_of_oil_equivalent "Barrel of oil equivalent") (12,000,000 GJ) of daily production.[[33]](#cite_note-wharton-34) On 1 May 2006, after the [Bolivian gas conflict](/wiki/Bolivian_gas_conflict "Bolivian gas conflict"), Bolivia's president [Evo Morales](/wiki/Evo_Morales "Evo Morales") announced the nationalization of all [gas and oil fields in the country](/wiki/Geology_of_Bolivia "Geology of Bolivia") and ordered the occupation of all fields by the [Bolivian Army](/wiki/Bolivian_Army "Bolivian Army").[[34]](#cite_note-35) On 4 May 2006, Petrobras cancelled a major future investment plan in Bolivia as a result.[[35]](#cite_note-36) The Bolivian government demanded an increase in royalty payments from foreign petroleum companies to 82%, but eventually settled for a 50% royalty interest.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

In 2007, Petrobras inaugurated the Petrobras 52 Oil Platform. The 52 is the biggest Brazilian oil platform and the third-biggest in the world.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

In 2007 and 2008, Petrobras made several major oil discoveries including the [Tupi oil field](/wiki/Tupi_oil_field "Tupi oil field") (formerly known as the Lula oil field), the [Jupiter field](/wiki/Jupiter_field "Jupiter field"), and the [Sugar Loaf field](/wiki/Sugar_Loaf_field "Sugar Loaf field"), all in the [Santos Basin](/wiki/Santos_Basin "Santos Basin"), 300 km off the coast of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). The oil fields were discovered by partnerships that include Petrobras, [Royal Dutch Shell](/wiki/Royal_Dutch_Shell "Royal Dutch Shell"), and [Galp Energia](/wiki/Galp_Energia "Galp Energia"). However, estimates for the reserves of these new fields varied widely.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Oil_platform_P-51_%28Brazil%29.jpg/250px-Oil_platform_P-51_%28Brazil%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oil_platform_P-51_(Brazil).jpg)

Oil platform P-51, the first 100% Brazilian oil platform

The P-51 Platform, the first semisubmersible platform built entirely in Brazil, capable of producing up to 180,000 barrels of oil per day, started production in the [Campos Basin](/wiki/Campos_Basin "Campos Basin") in January 2009,[[39]](#cite_note-40) and in February 2009, China agreed to loan Petrobras US$10 billion in exchange for a supply of 60,000–100,000 barrels of oil per day to a subsidiary of [Sinopec](/wiki/Sinopec "Sinopec") and 40,000-60,000 barrels of oil per day to [PetroChina](/wiki/PetroChina "PetroChina").[[40]](#cite_note-41) In August 2009, Petrobras acquired [ExxonMobil](/wiki/ExxonMobil "ExxonMobil")'s [Esso](/wiki/Esso "Esso") assets in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") for US$400 million.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

In September 2010, Petrobras completed a US$70 billion share offering, the largest share offering in history, to be used to develop newly discovered oil fields.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Giovanni Biscardi and Machado Meyer represented Petrobras. Biscardi brought his Brazilian corporate practice to [Greenberg Traurig](/wiki/Greenberg_Traurig "Greenberg Traurig") in January 2020.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

In 2012, Petrobras surrendered permits to explore offshore in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). Petrobras did not provide a reason but the New Zealand Prime Minister [John Key](/wiki/John_Key "John Key") said the decision was "not a reflection on the capacity to undertake deep-sea drilling or the prospect of activity of that area". He attributed the decision to a regrouping by the company after some setbacks.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

In July 2013, a worker [strike action](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") shut down production at several of the company's oil platforms.[[45]](#cite_note-46) In September 2013, Petrobras sold eleven onshore exploration and production blocks in [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") to [Perenco](/wiki/Perenco "Perenco") for US$380 million.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

In September 2013 [Organizações Globo](/wiki/Organiza%C3%A7%C3%B5es_Globo "Organizações Globo") reported on national television that the US [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency") (NSA) had been spying on Petrobras. The information was based on a top secret NSA file provided to *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* journalist [Glenn Greenwald](/wiki/Glenn_Greenwald "Glenn Greenwald") by [Edward Snowden](/wiki/Edward_Snowden "Edward Snowden") as part of the [Global surveillance disclosures](/wiki/Global_surveillance_disclosures_(2013%E2%80%93present) "Global surveillance disclosures (2013–present)"). The file showed that Petrobras was one of several targets for the NSA's Blackpearl program, which extricates data from private networks.[[47]](#cite_note-48) Petrobras announced that it was investing R$21 billion over five years to improve its data security.[[48]](#cite_note-data_security_2013-49)

In 2014, the company sold its assets in [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") to [PetroChina](/wiki/PetroChina "PetroChina") for US$2.6 billion.[[49]](#cite_note-50) Also in 2014, Petrobras set a new company record for average daily production of 2.863 million [barrels of oil equivalent](/wiki/Barrel_of_oil_equivalent "Barrel of oil equivalent") (17,520,000 GJ).[[50]](#cite_note-51)

In January 2017, the company concluded the sale of 100 percent of Petrobras Chile Distribuición Ltda (PCD) to the [Southern Cross Group](/wiki/Southern_Cross_Group_(Australia) "Southern Cross Group (Australia)"). The transaction included the licensing of the Petrobras and Lubrax brands for 8 years. To operate the assets acquired from Petrobras in Chile, Southern Cross created Esmax, a company that acts as a Petrobras licensee in the fuel and lubricant distribution segments. In March 2019, the company concluded the sale of 100 percent of Petrobras Paraguay Distribución Limited (PPDL UK), Petrobras Paraguay Operaciones y Logística SRL (PPOL) and Petrobras Paraguay Gas SRL (PPG) to the Grupo Copetrol, through its subsidiary Paraguay Energy. The agreement provides for the licensing for the exclusive use of the Petrobras brand by Nextar (the successor of Petrobras Paraguay Operaciones y Logística SRL) at that country's service stations, for the initial term of five years. In February 2021, the company concluded the sale of entire stake in Petrobras Uruguay Distribución S.A. (PUDSA), by indirect subsidiary (Petrobras Uruguay Sociedad Anónima de Inversión -PUSAI), in Uruguay, to Mauruguay S.A., an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Disa Corporación Petrolífera S.A. (DISA).[[51]](#cite_note-52)

In January 2020, Petroleo Brasileiro stated that it ended all of its business in Africa after completing the sale of a 50% stake in Petrobras Oil & Gas BV.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

### Ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ownership")]

The Brazilian government directly owns 54% of Petrobras' [common shares](/wiki/Common_stock "Common stock") with voting rights, while the [Brazilian Development Bank](/wiki/Brazilian_Development_Bank "Brazilian Development Bank") and [Brazil's Sovereign Wealth Fund (Fundo Soberano)](/wiki/Sovereign_Fund_of_Brazil "Sovereign Fund of Brazil") each control 5%, bringing the State's direct and indirect ownership to 64%.[[53]](#cite_note-54) The privately held shares are traded on [B3](/wiki/B3_(stock_exchange) "B3 (stock exchange)"), where they are part of the [Ibovespa](/wiki/%C3%8Dndice_Bovespa "Índice Bovespa") index.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is also listed in the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") in the form of [American depositary receipts](/wiki/American_depositary_receipt "American depositary receipt") and in the [Madrid Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bolsa_de_Madrid "Bolsa de Madrid").

## Operation Car Wash and related protests in Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Operation Car Wash and related protests in Brazil")]

See also: [Operation Car Wash](/wiki/Operation_Car_Wash "Operation Car Wash"), [2015–2016 protests in Brazil](/wiki/2015%E2%80%932016_protests_in_Brazil "2015–2016 protests in Brazil"), and [Offshoots of Operation Car Wash](/wiki/Offshoots_of_Operation_Car_Wash "Offshoots of Operation Car Wash")

**Operation Car Wash** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): ***Operação Lava Jato***) was a criminal investigation by the [Federal Police of Brazil](/wiki/Federal_Police_of_Brazil "Federal Police of Brazil")'s [Curitiba](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba") branch. Originally a [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering") investigation, it expanded to cover allegations of corruption at Petrobras, where executives allegedly accepted [bribes](/wiki/Bribe "Bribe") in return for awarding contracts to construction firms at inflated prices.[[60]](#cite_note-61) The aim of the investigation was to ascertain the extent of a money laundering scheme, estimated by the Regional Superintendent of the Federal Police of [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_(state) "Paraná (state)") State in 2015 at [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")6.4–42.8 billion (US$2–13 billion), largely through the [embezzlement](/wiki/Embezzlement "Embezzlement") of Petrobras funds.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-FinancialReport2015-63): 60

The authorities issued over a thousand warrants against business figures and politicians.[[63]](#cite_note-64) It also led to a wave of arrests. Fernando Soares, also known as "Fernando Baiano," a businessman and lobbyist, was allegedly the connection between major Brazilian construction firms and the government formed by the [Workers’ Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)")(PT) and [Brazilian Democratic Movement](/wiki/Brazilian_Democratic_Movement "Brazilian Democratic Movement") (PMDB).[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-ab1-66) Between 2014 and February 2016, the [Federal Public Prosecutor's Office](/wiki/Public_Prosecutor%27s_Office_(Brazil) "Public Prosecutor's Office (Brazil)") (Portuguese: *Ministério Público da União*) filed 37 criminal charges against 179 people, mostly politicians and businessmen.[[66]](#cite_note-Fausto_2017-67) Former President [Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva](/wiki/Luiz_In%C3%A1cio_Lula_da_Silva "Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva")[[67]](#cite_note-68) and then President [Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Dilma_Rousseff "Dilma Rousseff")[[68]](#cite_note-69) were also implicated.

On 8 March 2016, [Marcelo Odebrecht](/wiki/Marcelo_Odebrecht "Marcelo Odebrecht"), CEO of [Odebrecht](/wiki/Odebrecht "Odebrecht") and grandson of the company's founder, was sentenced to 19 years in prison after being convicted of paying more than $30 million in bribes to Petrobras executives.[[69]](#cite_note-70) [Eduardo Cunha](/wiki/Eduardo_Cunha "Eduardo Cunha"), president of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Brazil) "Chamber of Deputies (Brazil)") from 2015 to 2016, was convicted of taking approximately $40 million in bribes and hiding funds in secret bank accounts and sentenced to 15 years in prison.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Brazil_protest_2016_March.jpg/250px-Brazil_protest_2016_March.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brazil_protest_2016_March.jpg)

[Protests in Brazil](/wiki/2015%E2%80%932016_protests_in_Brazil "2015–2016 protests in Brazil") in 2015–2016 were sparked by revelations that a number of politicians had accepted bribes linked to contracts at Petrobras.

Protests broke out calling for the resignation or [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Dilma_Rousseff "Impeachment of Dilma Rousseff") of President [Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Dilma_Rousseff "Dilma Rousseff"). The most widespread of these occurred on 13 March 2016 in over 300 municipalities. Police estimates gave about 3.5 million protestors throughout the country.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Some of the protests were in areas previously thought of as strongholds of the [Workers Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)"), of which Rousseff was the leader.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

The [Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation](/wiki/Bill_and_Melinda_Gates_Foundation "Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation") sued Petrobras and its auditors, [PriceWaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PriceWaterhouseCoopers "PriceWaterhouseCoopers") as a result of the corruption scandal.[[74]](#cite_note-75) In January 2018, Petrobras agreed to pay $2.95 billion to settle a U.S. class action corruption lawsuit.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Later in September 2018, Petrobras agreed to pay $853.2 million to settle with [Brazilian](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") authorities.[[76]](#cite_note-77) Petrobras settled with shareholder [Vanguard Group](/wiki/The_Vanguard_Group "The Vanguard Group") in June 2017.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

On 1 February 2023 the company said it received R $132 million, as victim-beneficiary of the award-winning collaboration agreement signed between the Federal Prosecutor's Office and Rogério Santos de Araújo, before the Supreme Court.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

## Environmental record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Environmental record")]

Petrobras's website notes several initiatives to preserve the environment. These include efforts to support both ocean and forest ecosystems.[[79]](#cite_note-80) Most notably, Petrobras has sponsored [population studies](/wiki/Population_studies "Population studies") and conservation efforts for [humpback whales](/wiki/Humpback_whale "Humpback whale") in northeast Brazil. The company's efforts helped to rebuild Brazil's humpback whale populations from 2,000 in the mid-nineties to over 9,000 in 2008.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

Petrobras subscribes to the [United Nations Global Compact](/wiki/United_Nations_Global_Compact "United Nations Global Compact"), a voluntary agreement regarding human rights, [working conditions](/wiki/Working_condition "Working condition"), [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption"), and the [environment](/wiki/Environment_(biophysical) "Environment (biophysical)").[[81]](#cite_note-82)

In 2008, the Spanish consultancy firm Management and Excellence named Petrobras the world's most [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainable "Sustainable") oil company.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

## Sponsorships and namesakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petrobras&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sponsorships and namesakes")]

* In the *[Speed Racer](/wiki/Speed_Racer_(film) "Speed Racer (film)")* live-action movie, one of the cars featured is the "Green Energy", a [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel")-fueled racing car sponsored by Petrobras.[[84]](#cite_note-85)
* Petrobras sponsored the [Brazilian Série A](/wiki/Brazilian_S%C3%A9rie_A "Brazilian Série A") from 2009 to 2012.[[85]](#cite_note-86)
* Petrobras was a secondary sponsor for the [AT&T Williams F1 Team](/wiki/Williams_Grand_Prix_Engineering "Williams Grand Prix Engineering") from 1999 to 2008 (2000 to 2006 was as only fuel supplier due to Williams had a [Castrol](/wiki/Castrol "Castrol") lubrication partnership and later increased role to lubricant supplier from 2007 to 2008) and signed again with Williams F1 from 2014 onwards.[[86]](#cite_note-87) From 2018, Petrobras left Williams and sponsored [McLaren](/wiki/McLaren "McLaren"), but cancelled their sponsorship at the end of 2019.
* Petrobras sponsored the [Clube de Regatas do Flamengo](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo") in Brazil from 1984 to 2009, [Racing Club de Avellaneda](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Avellaneda "Racing Club de Avellaneda") from 2002 to 2006, [Club Atlético River Plate](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_River_Plate "Club Atlético River Plate") in Argentina from 2006 to 2012 and [Universidad de Chile](/wiki/Club_Universidad_de_Chile "Club Universidad de Chile") in Chile from 2019 to 2021.[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91)
* The [sauropod](/wiki/Sauropod "Sauropod") [dinosaur](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur") *[Petrobrasaurus](/wiki/Petrobrasaurus "Petrobrasaurus")* is named after the company.[[91]](#cite_note-92)
* Petrobras sponsored the game *Copa Petrobras de Marcas*, an unfinished version of *Stock Car Extreme* by Reiza Studios, the creators of the *Automobilista* series.
